text: the insistent buzzing, and Forgotten Cove presented itself. I regained my feet and stumbled through the woods until I hit sand, then continued until the sand turned dark with seawater. I fell to my knees and a wave soaked my pants. Laughter bubbled through the exhaustion, fear, and confusion. I gripped big handfuls of wet sand, trying to ground myself in the reality of land and sea. The breeze was cool again. The buzzing only that of summertime insects. In a fit of joy, I stood and tore off my shirt, the delicious sea calling to me. Faint blue and red lights sparkled in the crystalline water. I kicked off my shoes and removed my pants. Broken shells bit my feet. Waves licked my ankles, then my shins. The teasing tide inflamed my desire to be fully immersed and protected from impudent sun. Behind me, a voice halted my progress. “I wouldn’t do that if I were you.” The owner of the pursuing footsteps had found me. # Vanessa watched curiously as I disrobed, removing my shirt, pants, shoes, and socks. I bared all of my crosshatched scars. Countless cuts and countless screams. She bit her lip, my scarred body a promise. “What about your underwear?” “Not necessary.” Her expression collapsed. She eyed the container that again appeared as if it only held water. Then she looked at the chest of surgical tools, frowning. “Listen—” “I need your help with this.” “Help with what ? Are we going to fuck?” “No.” “Then what the hell were you doing on a dating site?” “A fetish… extreme fetish dating site.” “What’s your point?” She threw her hands in the air. “I’m not screwing a sea monster, man.” “That’s not—” “Then what? Spill it or I’m gone. And I’m taking my too spicy bag of food.” “Okay.” I held my hands up in surrender. “Give me five minutes. I’ll explain, and then if you want to leave, you can leave. Food and all.” I carried the container to the wood chair and sat. “Deal?” Vanessa looked around my place, maybe thinking she could squeeze me for some cash if she stayed. Maybe just curious about what I had in mind. She retrieved her food from the counter and sat on a stool. Digging into the bag, she said, “You’re lucky I’m hungry.” # I turned to face my pursuer. The stocky man wearing forest green chest waders placed a metal bucket onto the sand. “Step out of the water.” “Why are you following me?” I backed up. My state of undress made me feel vulnerable, but the tone of the man’s voice twisted that vulnerability into all-out fear. “Please. You need to step out of the—” “Ouch!” A searing pain shot through my leg, as if a molten thread of sugar had dripped on me. The man’s eyes filled with fear. He grabbed my arm and pulled me toward shore. I resisted at first, but another line of fire ignited my leg. Then another, and another, until my vision went dull and gray. I couldn’t hear the man anymore. His words were a distorted warbling. The water and sand turned to slime, oozing between my toes, coating my feet with an infected bisque. Traces of fire extended to my thighs, winding around my muscles and tendons, like being bound by barbed wire. Summer sounds buzzed impatient and aggressive. I stumbled and the man tried to hold on to me, but his hands were barely there. The cove, the sky, the world was barely there. A multitude of disparate shapes and colors took its place. Things with no context. My mind attempted to make sense of what I saw and regurgitated an aneurysm of words. A gurgling range of ship-ended wafers. Coagulated sonorous beacons attempting flight. Grim drapery of fleshy wings dripped from the sky. Atomized extensions of brief granite took a stumbling step skyward. Every breath was a nightmare. I inhaled liquid fire. My skin bubbled in clusters of melting fat, basting in my own juices. I screamed inhuman. My ears popped and warm liquid poured out, dripping over my jawline. The next scream was terrible silence. Something I couldn’t see tugged my arm and I fell to all fours on a rubber ground crawling with biting things. I had no name for the flat corpuscular monsters. Something tugged at my legs. I collapsed onto my stomach and the ravenous creatures feasted. I rolled in an attempt to dislodge them but fell motionless and utterly dumbstruck. A sharp pain shot through my leg. My new hell flickered. Another cut and Forgotten Cove returned. The man who’d been following me was crouched at my bleeding legs with a knife in one gloved hand and a cluster of translucent, rubbery tendrils in the other. A quivering mass of blubber lay on the sand, a multitude of clustered protrusions convulsing in an epileptic jitterbug. I kicked away, sand flying around me, the sky spinning. “Stay put!” The man dropped the remains of the creature onto the sand and grabbed his metal bucket. He rushed over and thrust it into my hands. He winked. “I’m Benny.” I replied by heaving my guts into the container. A great gushing flood of chunky black and green ichor poured from me. It tasted of acidic sea. Like metallic rot. The torrent continued into absurdity, filling the large bucket with thick sludge. When I finally finished, Benny smiled and said, “Nice to meet you to.” Wiping my mouth, I stared at what had come out of me with disgust. I spit a few times into the sand before I was able to speak, and my first words were about as eloquent as I could muster. “What the fuck? Benny retrieved what looked like a huge digital thermometer and placed it into my vomit. “Ph Meter,” he said as if the explanation would make sense.
